Webpack is a pretty common web technology — it bundles up everything your site needs and packages it for delivery. So let me start logging my Webpack learning notes here.
Writing Vue CLI Online With CodeSandbox
We usually code in CodePen, JSBin, or JSFiddle, but none of those let you write a full Vue CLI app. CodeSandbox does — so let's take Vue CLI online! (~~feels like an ad~~)
Controlling Firestore With Private Rules (Firebase Admin Setup)

Last post showed how to connect Koa.js to Firestore, but it had a serious security issue — the rules were public. This post shows how to lock the rules down and control Firestore via Firebase admin credentials.
Adding Data to Firestore With Koa.js
Had to set up Firestore for a work project. There's already some material out there but I'm writing my own notes anyway.
Subtitling a video is painful — you have to keep replaying to catch the timing. But if a tool can auto-generate 50% of the subtitle blocks for you, the rest is easy. Notes on how to set it up.

Work needs me to pick up Koa.js, so I'm setting it up to play around. My manager says it's basically like Express, but I still need to touch it myself to understand why. The framework uses a lot of async/sync patterns and mostly ES6 / ES7, so Express devs can transition without issues.
JavaScript async: The Other Half of async/await

Last time I covered await, so naturally async is up next. Wherever there's synchronous code there's asynchronous code too, so here I'm jotting down some notes on how the async keyword works in JavaScript.
JavaScript await: How to Force Your Code to Wait Its Turn

We've already covered Promises, so the next thing on the list is async and await. I'm splitting those two into separate notes, and this post is all about await — what it does, and how it stops one sneaky line of code from running before everything else is ready.
Async vs Sync in JavaScript: Getting to Know Promise

async and await are everyday tools in JavaScript, and they're tied closely to Promise. So before we get into them, let's start with the basics: what a Promise actually is, how sync and async events work, and why Callback Hell is such a pain.
[JS Weird World] Bonus: Closures, One More Time
JavaScript Full Guide: Conquering the Weird Parts of JS

I already covered closures earlier in this series, but honestly I still felt half-sure about them. So I pulled the closure topic out into its own bonus episode to dig in a bit more. This post walks through what a closure is, why you'd want one, and a money-tracking example that shows why inner variables stick around instead of getting wiped out.
